text.content:
The following is a sub-system for emulating tactical grid-based battles using a plug-n-play approach to convert characters into "tactica" units through the use of active effects and certain rules covered in this journal

Many of its design decisions are inspired by games dear to me. I have tried my best to translate those mechanics into this system without over complicating things for myself or my players.

It has been designed to emulate small skirmishes and army battles using archetypes and tropes common to the SRPG genre. So rather than taking your existing PC and NPC sheets into the system I would encourage you to create new ones following the unit building rules or start from one of the provided templates.

All that said, feel free to modify whatever you see here for your own table!

The following table summarizes the unit types to be used in the sub-system and their key characteristics. To convert any given unit to that type, just add the corresponding effect to it.

Each unit types has certain adjustments made to it, with some being particularly vulnerable to certain bane-type weapons.

Some weapon types have boast particular advantages and disadvantages against others. These are not designed to provide hard counters but instead add a layer of tactical depth and planning when setting up specific match-ups.

The following are specific traits and keywords used in the subsystem.

Term

Description

Unit

An unit is a character that takes part in a combat within this subsystem. It can represent a single person or a group of people as part of a larger force.

Chain Attack

If the unit is adjacent to an enemy when an ally performs a melee attack, the unit with this feature can perform an HR0 attack before the initiating attack.

Chain Guard

If the unit is not in crisis, has waited and is adjacent to an ally when an enemy performs a melee attack, the unit will take the damage in place of their ally.

Terrain Bonus

When the unit ends its turn under certain types of terrain, gains increased defenses until the start of its next turn.

Break

When attacking an unit, if the result of the accuracy check was an even number, it cannot counter.

Bane

When attacking an unit of a specific type, bonus damage is dealt.

When a combat using this sub-system takes place, the following rules are in place

Turn Order

Each factions' units take their turns one after another during that faction's phase. Each combat round begins with a Player Phase followed by the Enemy Phase, thus ending the round.

During a phase, the units take their turns based on their overall agility, which can be represented by initiative or their dexterity attribute. If there's a tie you can let players quickly decide amongst themselves which units go first.

Don't over complicate this and just make a quick call.

Unit Actions

Each units turn consists of a movement phase followed by an action phase. Note that all movement and action ranges is tied along the cardinal directions (left, up, down, right).

Movement Phase

The unit can move up to their movement range. Some units such as flying can cross all terrain such as water, mountains, etc.

Action Phase

Second, it can perform an action:

  • Attack: It can perform an attack on another unit within their attack range along the 4 cardinal directions. (If you are not familiar with the genre, an orthogonal would take one extra unit of range). The targeted unit may then counter.

  • Skill: Some units have special skills they can perform on their turn.

  • Wait: The unit ends their turn.

Counters

When an unit attacks another it usually provokes a counter attack.

  • Melee weapons usually provoke counters unless specified otherwise.

  • Bows cannot counter attacks with melee weapons by default.

  • When an unit inflicts break on another, it will provoke no counters.

  • Some skills affect the counter rules, on a case-by-case basis.

Resolution

The combat typically ends when one faction wipes out the other, or a certain objective is accomplished.

Death

Depending on the tone of your campaign and the importance of the players' units, the following can happen when they would reach 0 Hit Points.

  • Generic units are killed immediately.

  • Named units are injured and immediately retreat from the combat.

  • Named units are incapacitated and cannot take action for the rest of the combat unless they are healed, at which point they must wait out their next turn. There has to be a penalty!

With the sub-system still being play tested and being largely an experimental endeavor there will be certain decisions and adjustments that will need to be made as time goes on. Changes from versions will be recorded here for posterity.

Version

Changes

1

-

In this sub-system, units can have the following passive and active skills. Here are some examples.

Passive Skills

  • @UUID[Compendium.fu-azure-compendia.skills.Item.y2o9WAL7YH2LLaqG]{Tactica: Pass}

  • @UUID[Compendium.fu-azure-compendia.skills.Item.9ti16lwufANkmqDO]{Tactica: Reprisal}

  • @UUID[Compendium.fu-azure-compendia.skills.Item.eGra15bE3PeEhf5x]{Tactica: Canter}

  • @UUID[Compendium.fu-azure-compendia.skills.Item.A67rjtmZgi1hdSHf]{Tactica: Vantage}

  • @UUID[Compendium.fu-azure-compendia.skills.Item.ne4lbhKBXxq6S51j]{Tactica: Renewal}

  • @UUID[Compendium.fu-azure-compendia.skills.Item.EVkrL6Qd314R3b11]{Tactica: Stealth}

  • @UUID[Compendium.fu-azure-compendia.skills.Item.AanUSekK2iaS3Ay4]{Tactica: Pathfinder}

Active Skills

  • @UUID[Compendium.fu-azure-compendia.skills.Item.KskESzu4oKqHSIaO]{Tactica: Pivot}

  • @UUID[Compendium.fu-azure-compendia.skills.Item.gsDqi5E9Go4dCdiE]{Tactica: Shove}

  • @UUID[Compendium.fu-azure-compendia.skills.Item.dH01wa9SzVmUIUh0]{Tactica: Rally Body}

  • @UUID[Compendium.fu-azure-compendia.skills.Item.iWwLsWDyzj10vDld]{Tactica: Rally Mind}

  • @UUID[Compendium.fu-azure-compendia.skills.Item.uCegTE5JLrff354q]{Tactica: Dance}

  • @UUID[Compendium.fu-azure-compendia.skills.Item.TJdztnKb5uajOhLk]{Tactica: Reposition}

  • @UUID[Compendium.fu-azure-compendia.skills.Item.GqpuUGOAmlJK8cPe]{Tactica: Run Through}

Spells

These can be treated as active skills that consume MP.

  • @UUID[Compendium.fu-azure-compendia.spells.Item.BAc5iA1FimrMbc1e]{Tactica Heal}

Unit Anatomy

A tactica unit is a simplified NPC which should have the following, that are implemented via effects on the actors or their weapons:

  • @UUID[Compendium.fu-azure-compendia.journals.JournalEntry.FHnjDvV196AmONvt.JournalEntryPage.1aKDL28nvM1Sn0s6]{Unit Type}: A mechanical representation of their archetype and key attributes.

  • @UUID[Compendium.fu-azure-compendia.journals.JournalEntry.FHnjDvV196AmONvt.JournalEntryPage.ovX25EkTqMzRIMWF]{Weapons}: For generic and enemy units, one weapon. For the players' named units, up to two weapons.

  • @UUID[Compendium.fu-azure-compendia.journals.JournalEntry.FHnjDvV196AmONvt.JournalEntryPage.iRvbxIuyaDgKpOEV]{Skills}: For important enemy units, one skill. For the player's named units they should start with one, and possibly unlock others.

Note that weapons have been implemented as NPC attacks to keep things simple. If your have characters with multiple "weapons" make sure they disable they toggle the weapon effects accordingly.

Spaces

The system is meant to be played in a grid, with each cell in the grid being referred to as a space within these rules.

When counting spaces for the purposes of movement and action ranges, you will want to count along the cardinal directions.

For example, moving to an unit's diagonal would take up two movement points.

Actors

The Characters compendium has been updated to include a folder with sample units for you to use as a base or out of the box. There are two categories:

  • Heroes: These would be the characters your players would use in the encounters with the subsystem.

  • Units: These are a collection of archetypes you can use.

Sprites

The sprites included in the module for use in the units have been re-scaled to 256x256 since quite a few units don't have an uniform size. The prototype tokens have an image scale of 2 at the minimum, with some being higher.

Maps

A variety of maps have been included for quick use with the system before you either look up or make up your own.

This page will list some not-so-quick napkin math for balancing your map and some scenario templates.

  • Deployment Count: For each player two distinct units to control, starting with just one for the introduction to the system, up to a limit of 12 units. (If you are one of those crazy ambitious people with 9 players, maybe stick to just one each).

  • Enemy Count: For each unit deployed, at least 2 enemy units on the map.

Guidelines

  • For ambushes keep the initial count of enemy units equal to that of the deployed units, then spawn reinforcements two turns later or after all the initial enemies are defeated.

  • For bigger maps, space enemy groups of at least 1/2 DC apart so that there's a one-turn distance before the next group to give the player units a chance to recover.

  • Enemy units should respond sensibly and rarely overwhelmingly (at least to start with). You need to keep up the pressure on the player units with regular engagements to keep up the tension without leading to a death-spiral where their units can't keep up.
